ground_truth.title: Is a Medical Billing Bootcamp or Traditional Program For You?
ground_truth.author: All Allied Health Staff
ground_truth.main_content:
Whether you're just entering the workforce, returning after a break or transitioning careers, pursuing a career in medical billing and coding may be right for you. Jobs in medical billing and coding allow you to work in the dynamic and growing field of healthcare without being involved in direct patient care. Instead, you can make a meaningful contribution to the well-being of others by ensuring that healthcare providers are properly reimbursed for services and that patients receive the insurance benefits they need.

A medical coding bootcamp is a short-term intensive program that focuses on the skills you need to work in the healthcare industry. Like other bootcamps in the computer and information technology fields, medical coding bootcamps are concentrated programs that teach essential skills and provide hands-on training in the field. The purpose of a medical coding bootcamp is to help you earn the skills that employers desire without spending the additional time and money needed to earn a college degree. The condensed and fast-paced format of a coding bootcamp means that you may be able to complete your education in six to 12 weeks, depending on the bootcamp format and timing.

Traditional medical coding programs can include an associate or bachelor's degree in related areas such as computer science, forensics, health information management, or healthcare administration. A degree program offers a broader educational experience that includes coursework in areas such as liberal arts and the general sciences in addition to studies related to medical billing and coding and the overall healthcare system. While this multi-disciplinary approach gives you the skills you need to succeed in a broader variety of career options within the healthcare field, completing these programs requires more time and money than bootcamps.

The decision to pursue a medical coding bootcamp over a traditional education is a personal decision based on your needs and professional goals. A traditional program may be right for you if your long-term goals involve pursuing more advanced roles in the healthcare revenue cycle with responsibilities beyond medical billing and coding.

Factors to consider when choosing include accreditation, financial aid eligibility, and professional goals. Most bootcamps are not affiliated with a college or university and are not accredited since accreditation is typically reserved for higher education institutions. However, bootcamps that are not sponsored by a college or university do not qualify for accreditation by the Department of Education and are not eligible to participate in federally funded student aid or grant programs.
